quotes.text: Both the priest and the Levite in Christ’s parable should have remembered the requirements of the law: “Thou shalt not see thy brother’s ass or his ox fall down by the way, and hide thyself from them: thou shalt surely help him to lift them up again” (Deuteronomy 22:4). And if an ox, how much more should one be willing to help a brother in need. But as Elder James E. Talmage wrote, “Excuses [not to do so] are easy to find; they spring up as readily and plentifully as weeds by the wayside” (Jesus the Christ, 3rd ed. [Salt Lake City: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, 1916], p. 431).
quotes.speaker: Howard W. Hunter
quotes.attr: Howard W. Hunter, in Conference Report, Oct. 1986, 43; or Ensign, Nov. 1986, 34

quotes.text: The thrust of this passage is that the mother bird should be left free to produce more young; that is, one should not destroy the breeding stock. The counsel to preserve bird life carries the observation, "that it may be well with thee, and that thou mayest prolong thy days." The inference is that prolonging human life on a long-term basis requires wise management of the available resources of the land.
quotes.speaker: Robert J. Matthews
quotes.source: What the Scriptures Say About: Ecology
quotes.attr: Robert J. Matthews, "What the Scriptures Say About: Ecology," New Era, Mar. 1972, 39

quotes.text: Most roofs were flat and were made from beams anchored in the walls and covered with pine planks. The roof builders would lay heath, reeds, or palm leaves over the planks and cover the whole with clay, using small stone rollers to press it down. A layer of lime made the roof somewhat waterproof. The Mosaic law required that a parapet wall be placed around the perimeter of the roof for protection. (See Deut. 22:8.) Small holes in the parapet allowed rain to run off. The roof was considered part of the living area. It provided a place to dry clothes, fruits, and vegetables, to store wood for the winter, to nap, and to pray … In the evening, family members could congregate on the roof and take advantage of cool breezes and they often slept there during warm weather. One disadvantage of living on the roof was that animated conversation and arguments could be heard by neighbors. Considered in this light, the Lord's warning, "That which ye have spoken in the ear in closets shall be proclaimed upon the housetops" (Luke 12:3), takes on a potent meaning.
quotes.speaker: Richard D. Draper
quotes.attr: Richard D. Draper, "Home Life at the Time of Christ," Ensign, Sept. 1987, 56

quotes.text: The roof in biblical culture Most of the annual rainfall in biblical Israel and surrounding areas occurred during a relatively short period from early December to early March. Even during this period the rains were seldom as heavy as would be quite common in other parts of the world. The average annual rainfall in Israel today is less than 20 inches (50 cm), while even Nairobi has nearly twice as much rain with 38 inches (95 cm) per year. Sao Paulo and Bangkok average more than 57 inches (143 cm) per year, and Manila is reported to have 82 inches (205 cm) annually. Because of the scarcity of rainfall in the eastern Mediterranean area, it was - and still is - possible to have flat roofs in that part of the world. In biblical times, the roof was usually built on heavy beams often made of sycamore and supported by columns. These beams were overlaid with branches and twigs and then secured with mud or clay. Then a heavy roller was used to pack the earthen covering. Following a rainy period the roof would be repaired by adding more earthen material and again compacted using the roller that was usually stored on the roof for this purpose. In addition to being flat, there are certain other characteristics of the biblical roof that differ from the housetop in many other cultures. Because it was flat, the roof was used by people in ways that could not be imagined in parts of the world where flat roofs are unknown. The people of Israel were instructed to build a kind of guard rail around the edge of the roof of their homes (Dt 22.8) in order that they might not be held accountable if a neighbor or visitor were to fall from their housetop. In some cases the roof referred to in the Bible was spacious and very strong. In the story of Samson we learn that a roof might hold as many as three thousand people (Judges 16.27). Because it was flat and made of mud or clay, the roof was also a place where sprigs of grass might grow for a while, probably in corners and around edges where people would not often walk. The roof was sometimes used for the drying and storage of grain and this would explain where some of the seed to produce grass might have come from. In the cool of the evening it would not be unusual for the entire family living in a given house to go up to the roof and enjoy the cool evening breeze as they socialized with other families on neighboring roofs. Sometimes announcements would be made to the neighborhood at this time. And if something urgent came up a person might go up to the housetop at any time of day or night to shout the news. In some cases a kind of "penthouse" or "upper room" would be built on top of the roof. This "roof chamber" is mentioned especially in the story of the left-handed hero, Ehud, in Jg 3, as well as in 2 Kg 4.10 which describes the construction of such a room on the roof for the visiting prophet, Elisha. The "upper room" in which Jesus and his disciples met (Mk 14 and Lk 22) was probably a later extension and elaboration of the same sort of construction. This, in fact, may have been the beginning of the modern multi-story building.
quotes.speaker: John Ellington
quotes.attr: John Ellington, The Bible Translator, April 1990, 41:2, pp. 238-239

quotes.text: The scriptures tell us that virtue is to be cherished, that a virtuous woman is loved of the Lord. And as far back as the Mosaic Law the scriptures discuss the idea that when a woman is the unwilling victim of rape, she is innocent of crime; she is innocent of sin.
quotes.speaker: Rex D. Pinegar
quotes.attr: Rex D. Pinegar, "Let God Judge between Me and Thee," Ensign, Oct. 1981, 34

notes: "In connection with the seduction of a virgin … two, or really three, cases are distinguished; viz. (1)

Those who had undergone sexual mutilation, who were illegitimate children, or who were Ammonites or Moabites were not allowed to be part of "the congregation of the Lord," even to the tenth generation (vs. 2). One possible explanation for this prohibition is the following: "There seems to be some corruption of rules here, as contradictions to many of these can be found elsewhere in the Scriptures" (Rasmussen, Introduction to the Old Testament, 1:133). Ruth, a Moabitess, is just one of these examples. Another possible explanation is that the word congregation had a special, limited meaning. It referred to
